params.text:
The care worker's role — and where it stops

Care workers support people with medicines in many ways. Some tasks are low-risk and can be done with basic training; others are higher-risk and need specific training and a competency sign-off.

  • Prompting — reminding someone it is time to take their medicine.
  • Assisting — helping someone who directs the task, for example opening a bottle or passing a glass of water.
  • Administering — giving the medicine to the person, including selecting the dose from packaging and recording it. This is the highest-risk task and requires training and a competency assessment.

Knowing which of these you are doing — and staying within it — keeps both you and the person you support safe.

params.question: A person you support asks you to give them a tablet from their blister pack. You have completed the awareness course but your employer has not yet assessed your competency to administer. What should you do?

answers.text: Decline to administer, explain that you are not yet signed off, and ask a competency-assessed colleague or your manager to help.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Administering requires both training and a competency sign-off. Until then you must not do it, however simple it seems.

answers.text: Give the tablet, because a blister pack is easy and low-risk.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Ease is not the point — administering any medicine requires a competency assessment first.

answers.text: Take the tablet out and leave it on the table for them to take later.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. Leaving medicines out is unsafe and is not recorded properly. Get a competent colleague to support the person.

answers.text: Administer it just this once and tell your manager afterwards.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. There is no "just this once". You must be signed off as competent before administering at all.
